purpose: Defines accepted runtime spell JSON fields and enum values that the running game may load.

notes: This template mirrors the runtime JSON destination shape. It is checked against the structured template so fields cannot be added to one layer invisibly.
A field may be optional in individual spell JSON files, but if it appears here it must have a matching structured field unless it is explicitly runtime-only.

acceptedValueNotes.unlimited: Use `unlimited` only when the canonical spell prose allows any number of valid targets; do not use a fake high number such as 999.

fields.structuredLabel: Target Participation Requires Full Casting Range

log.why: Runtime targeting needs to know when selected targets must remain in range during a long casting before the spell can affect them.

fields.comment: Runtime targeting needs to know when selected targets must remain in range during a long casting before the spell can affect them. It maps to `targeting.targetParticipation.requiresWithinRangeForFullCasting`. It is populated from the structured markdown label `Target Participation Requires Full Casting Range`. Purpose: targeting.targetParticipation.requiresWithinRangeForFullCasting exists so canonical prose, structured markdown, and runtime JSON do not silently disagree about this mechanic. Use `true` when targets must remain within range for the whole casting time, and use `not_applicable` only when the field is carried as an explicit sentinel for spells without that mechanic.

log.why: Short target-participation notes preserve the exact long-casting wording beside the runtime flag.

fields.comment: Short target-participation notes preserve the exact long-casting wording beside the runtime flag. It maps to `targeting.targetParticipation.notes`. It is populated from the structured markdown label `Target Participation Notes`. Purpose: targeting.targetParticipation.notes exists so canonical prose, structured markdown, and runtime JSON do not silently disagree about the participation rule while the boolean field remains narrow.

log.why: Some multi-target spells require all selected targets to be within a shared distance of each other.

fields.comment: targeting.targetCluster stores mutual-distance rules for selected targets. It is populated from the Target Cluster structured fields. Omit it only when the spell has no rule such as targets being within 30 feet of each other.

log.why: The runtime needs to know whether a mutual-distance target rule is active.

fields.comment: targeting.targetCluster.requirement maps from Target Cluster Requirement. Use `required` when the spell's selected targets must satisfy the cluster distance; use `not_applicable` only as a sentinel.

log.why: The runtime needs the numeric target-cluster distance.

fields.comment: targeting.targetCluster.maxDistance maps from Target Cluster Max Distance. Use the canonical distance number, or `not_applicable` only when carrying a sentinel object.

log.why: The runtime needs a unit for the target-cluster distance.

fields.comment: targeting.targetCluster.distanceUnit maps from Target Cluster Distance Unit. Use `feet` for within-30-feet rules unless canonical prose uses a different unit.

log.why: The runtime needs to know which chosen targets the cluster rule covers.

fields.comment: targeting.targetCluster.scope maps from Target Cluster Scope. `all_targets` means every selected target must satisfy the mutual-distance rule.

log.why: Short notes preserve the exact target-cluster wording next to the typed fields.

fields.comment: targeting.targetCluster.notes maps from Target Cluster Notes. Keep this short and do not use it as a substitute for the typed requirement, maxDistance, distanceUnit, and scope fields.

log.why: Selected-area targeting needs a runtime object rather than overloading maxTargets or prose.

fields.comment: targeting.areaTargetSelection records caster-selected or otherwise filtered targets inside an area footprint. It is populated from the structured Area Target Selection fields. Omit it only when the spell has no selected-area targeting mechanic.

log.why: Area targeting must distinguish all-target areas from caster-selected targets inside the same footprint.

fields.comment: targeting.areaTargetSelection.mode maps from Area Target Selection Mode. Use `caster_choice` when the caster selects affected creatures inside the area, and `not_applicable` only when the object is carried as a sentinel.

log.why: The selected target population needs to be explicit for runtime targeting prompts.

fields.comment: targeting.areaTargetSelection.scope maps from Area Target Selection Scope. The current selected-area batch uses `creatures_in_area`.

log.why: Uncapped selected-area spells need a value that does not invent a target maximum.

fields.comment: targeting.areaTargetSelection.count maps from Area Target Selection Count. Use `all_chosen` for 'each creature of your choice' with no numeric cap, or a number when the canonical prose gives a cap.

log.why: Runtime targeting must know whether unchosen valid creatures inside the area are unaffected.

fields.comment: targeting.areaTargetSelection.excludesUnchosen maps from Area Target Selection Excludes Unchosen. Set true when valid creatures inside the area are ignored unless selected by the caster.

fields.structuredLabel: Area Target Selection Requires Line Of Sight

log.why: Some selected-area spells only let the caster choose creatures they can see.

fields.comment: targeting.areaTargetSelection.requiresLineOfSight maps from Area Target Selection Requires Line Of Sight. Use it when the selection itself carries a sight gate.

log.why: A short note can preserve local selection wording while the typed fields stay narrow.

fields.comment: targeting.areaTargetSelection.notes maps from Area Target Selection Notes and should stay short; it is not a replacement for the typed selected-area fields.

log.why: Repeated spell units assigned among targets need their own runtime object.

fields.comment: targeting.instanceAllocation records repeated spell-created units such as Magic Missile darts. It is populated from Target Instance structured fields. Omit it only when the spell has no assignable target instances.

log.why: The runtime needs to know what repeated spell unit is being assigned.

fields.comment: targeting.instanceAllocation.instanceType maps from Target Instance Type. Use `dart` for Magic Missile, `beam` for Eldritch Blast, `ray` for Scorching Ray, `projectile` for planted ammunition, `light` for Dancing Lights-style light instances, `animated_tree` for Druid Grove-style awakened-tree guardians, and `controlled_undead` for Animate Dead-style reasserted undead targets.

log.why: Assignable target instances need a base count before scaling.

fields.comment: targeting.instanceAllocation.baseCount maps from Target Instance Base Count and records how many assignable units the base spell creates.

log.why: Extra assignable units may scale differently from ordinary target count.

fields.comment: targeting.instanceAllocation.scalingRule maps from Target Instance Scaling Rule. Use a concise machine-readable phrase such as `slot_level_plus_one_per_level`.

log.why: The runtime needs to know whether repeated spell units can stack on one target or must be split.

fields.comment: targeting.instanceAllocation.assignment maps from Target Instance Assignment. Use `same_or_different_targets` when units can all hit one creature or be split among several. Use `independent_positions` when instances are placed or moved as separate points.

log.why: Simultaneous instance resolution can matter for downstream damage and trigger timing.

fields.comment: targeting.instanceAllocation.resolution maps from Target Instance Resolution. Use `simultaneous` when all assigned units strike at the same time and `persistent` when the instances remain for the duration.

log.why: Short allocation notes preserve wording without replacing typed allocation fields.

fields.comment: targeting.instanceAllocation.notes maps from Target Instance Notes and should stay short.

log.why: Targeting needs a place for choices assigned independently to each selected target.

fields.comment: targeting.perTargetChoice records choices that can be made separately for every selected target. It is populated from the Per Target Choice structured fields. Omit it only when the spell has no per-target choice mechanic.

log.why: The runtime needs to know which choice family is assigned per target.

fields.comment: targeting.perTargetChoice.choiceType maps from Per Target Choice Type. Use `ability` for Enhance Ability-style ability choices.

log.why: The runtime needs to know whether each target receives its own choice.

fields.comment: targeting.perTargetChoice.scope maps from Per Target Choice Scope. Use `each_target` when every selected target can receive an independent choice.

log.why: Runtime prompts need the allowed per-target options.

fields.comment: targeting.perTargetChoice.options[] maps from Per Target Choice Options and lists the allowed canonical choices for each target.

fields.structuredLabel: Per Target Choice Different Choices Allowed

log.why: Enhance Ability allows different selected targets to receive different ability choices.

fields.comment: targeting.perTargetChoice.differentChoicesAllowed maps from Per Target Choice Different Choices Allowed. Set true when targets can receive different choices.

log.why: The runtime needs to know whether the per-target choice is mandatory.

fields.comment: targeting.perTargetChoice.required maps from Per Target Choice Required. Set true when the spell cannot resolve without assigning the choice.

log.why: A short note preserves the exact per-target choice wording.

fields.comment: targeting.perTargetChoice.notes maps from Per Target Choice Notes and should stay short.

log.why: Follow-up damage can target a creature related to the first target instead of using the spell's initial targeting block.

fields.comment: effects[].secondaryTargeting records leap, chain, or other follow-up target relationships on the effect that uses them. It is populated from the Secondary Target structured fields. Omit it only when the effect has no secondary or chained target mechanic.

log.why: The runtime needs to know what event unlocks the secondary target.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Trigger maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.trigger. Use `primary_hit` for Green-Flame Blade-style hit riders, `duplicate_damage_die` for Chromatic Orb-style dice-match chains, and `not_applicable` only as a structured/template sentinel.

log.why: Secondary target distance is measured from an existing target, not from the caster.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Origin maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.origin. Use `primary_target` when the follow-up is measured from the initial hit target, and `previous_target` when each chain step is measured from the last target.

log.why: The secondary target relationship needs its own measured range.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Range maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.range. Store the numeric range from the secondary target origin, or `not_applicable` in structured/template review when there is no secondary target mechanic.

log.why: The secondary target range needs an explicit unit.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Range Unit maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.rangeUnit. Use `feet` for the current leap/chain spells.

log.why: The follow-up target can have a different eligibility rule than the primary spell target.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Valid Targets maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.validTargets. Use `creature` for Green-Flame Blade and Chromatic Orb unless canonical prose permits objects too.

log.why: Secondary targets need to say who chooses the follow-up target.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Selection maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.selection. Use `caster_choice` for current leap/chain spells.

log.why: Several follow-up mechanics require a different target from the origin target.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Must Be Different maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.mustBeDifferent. Use true when canonical prose says a different target or creature.

fields.structuredLabel: Secondary Target Requires Line Of Sight

log.why: Some follow-up targets require the caster to see the secondary creature.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Requires Line Of Sight maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.requiresLineOfSight. Use true only when the secondary target selection itself has a sight requirement.

log.why: Some chained targets require a fresh attack roll.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Requires Attack Roll maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.requiresAttackRoll. Use true for Chromatic Orb chain steps; use false for automatic rider damage such as Green-Flame Blade.

log.why: Some chained targets require a fresh damage roll.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Requires Damage Roll maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.requiresDamageRoll. Use true when canonical prose tells the caster to make a new damage roll.

log.why: Chromatic Orb-style chains can repeat based on slot level, while many secondary targets cannot repeat.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Repeat Rule maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.repeatRule. Use `none` for one-time secondary targets and `slot_level_max_leaps` when the maximum number of chains equals the slot level expended.

log.why: Repeating secondary target chains need an explicit maximum.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Max Leaps maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.maxLeaps. Use `slot_level` when the maximum equals the slot level expended, or `not_applicable` when the secondary target cannot repeat.

log.why: Some chains prohibit targeting the same creature more than once per casting.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Unique Per Casting maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.uniquePerCasting. Use true when canonical prose says each creature can be targeted only once by the casting.

log.why: Short notes preserve exact local wording while typed secondary target fields carry runtime behavior.

fields.comment: Secondary Target Notes maps to effects[].secondaryTargeting.notes. Keep this short and do not use it as a replacement for the typed secondary target fields.

log.why: Some spells have different effects on different later turns, which cannot be recovered from repeated turn_start triggers alone.

fields.comment: effectSchedule records turn- or phase-numbered spell stages. The actual damage, terrain, status, or utility payloads remain in `effects[]`; the schedule says when those payloads are active and points to them by index when possible. Omit it only when a spell has no staged effect schedule.

log.why: The runtime needs the schedule's overall timing family.

fields.comment: Effect Schedule Timing maps to `effectSchedule.timing`. Use `caster_later_turn_start` for schedules that advance at the start of the caster's later turns. `not_applicable` is a structured/template sentinel; omit the runtime object when no schedule applies.

log.why: Each staged turn window needs its own label, turn range, effect links, and target rule.

fields.comment: effectSchedule.entries[] maps from numbered Effect Schedule Entry structured fields. Each entry names a turn window and the effects active in that window, including target-count rules such as six different targets.

log.why: The template contract needs an explicit runtime path for the declared number of scheduled stages.

fields.comment: Effect Schedule Entry Count maps to the number of objects in `effectSchedule.entries[]`. It is used for review parity; the runtime stores the entries themselves rather than a separate count field.

log.why: Schedule notes clarify what is handled by the schedule versus normal immediate effects.

fields.comment: Effect Schedule Notes maps to `effectSchedule.notes`. Use it to state, for example, that initial on-cast damage is not part of a later-turn schedule.

fields.comment: Attack augments map from numbered Attack Augment structured fields. They record weapon prerequisites, spell-granted attacks, spellcasting ability substitution, damage die overrides, damage-type choices, and optional extra damage riders for weapon attacks modified by a spell. fields.comment: Ability check modifiers map from Ability Check Modifier structured fields. They record the affected check family, bonus dice or flat value, chosen-skill gating, frequency, and duration relationship for spells such as Guidance. Runtime mapping: `effects[].abilityCheckModifier`.

log.why: Mage Hand-style helper entities need runtime data instead of prose-only utility text.

fields.comment: Controlled entity data maps from Controlled Entity structured fields. It records the spell-created helper's type, count, control action, movement, leash distance, carry capacity, allowed interactions, prohibited actions, and ending triggers. Runtime mapping: `effects[].controlledEntity`.

log.why: Mode menus need first-class runtime metadata instead of being hidden in utility prose.

fields.comment: modeChoice records selectable spell operation menus. It does not replace `effects[]` or `controlOptions[]`; it points at them so the runtime can present and resolve the chosen operation.

log.why: The runtime needs to know the menu selection rule.

fields.comment: Mode Choice Type maps to `modeChoice.type`. Use `choose_one` for current menu spells; `not_applicable` is a structured sentinel, and runtime JSON should omit `modeChoice` when no mode menu exists.

log.why: The runtime needs each menu option as an indexed object with links to payload data.

fields.comment: modeChoice.options[] maps from numbered Mode Choice Option structured fields. Each option should name its canonical label and point at effect/control-option payload indexes when those payloads exist.

log.why: The declared option count makes missing menu entries visible during review.

fields.comment: Mode Choice Option Count maps to `modeChoice.optionCount` and should match the number of `modeChoice.options[]` entries.

log.why: The runtime needs to know when a mode can be selected or changed.

fields.comment: Mode Choice Timing maps to `modeChoice.timing`. Runtime JSON omits `modeChoice` when no menu exists; `not_applicable` is for structured/template review.

log.why: Mode menus can be backed by effects, utility control options, or both.

fields.comment: Mode Choice Options Source maps to `modeChoice.optionsSource` and explains where the option payloads live.

log.why: Some mode-menu spells limit active duration-based options.

fields.comment: Mode Choice Max Active Noninstantaneous maps to `modeChoice.maxActiveNonInstantaneous`.

log.why: The runtime needs to know whether active mode effects can be dismissed.

fields.comment: Mode Choice Can Dismiss Active maps to `modeChoice.canDismissActive`.

log.why: Short runtime notes preserve menu-level caveats.

fields.comment: Mode Choice Notes maps to `modeChoice.notes`.

log.why: Condition immunity needs its own runtime field instead of overloading damageType or Utility description text.

fields.comment: Stores named status conditions that a defensive effect prevents for its target. It maps from the structured markdown label `Condition Immunities` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].conditionImmunity[] exists so canonical prose, structured markdown, and runtime JSON do not silently disagree about condition-immunity mechanics. Use `damageType[]` for damage resistance or immunity, and use this field for condition names such as Frightened. Omit or use an empty array only when the defensive effect has no condition-immunity mechanic.

log.why: Non-condition prevention needs runtime data separate from damage and condition immunity.

fields.comment: Stores non-condition mechanics that a defensive effect prevents. It maps from the structured markdown label `Prevention Immunities` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].preventionImmunity[] preserves mechanics such as Aura of Life preventing hit point maximum reduction without flattening them into utility descriptions.

log.why: Exception-based resistance and immunity need a runtime path that keeps the broad damage set and its carve-outs machine-readable.

fields.comment: Stores damage types that are excluded from a broad defensive damage entry. It maps from the structured markdown label `Damage Type Exclusions` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].excludedDamageType[] preserves mechanics such as `all damage except Psychic` without turning the exception into free text. Use this alongside effects[].damageType[]; omit or use an empty array only when the defensive effect has no damage-type exception mechanic.

log.why: Dynamic defensive damage types need runtime data that distinguishes eligible options from the one active type.

fields.comment: Stores how a defensive effect chooses the active damage type from effects[].damageType[]. It maps from the structured markdown label `Defense Damage Type Source` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].damageTypeSource keeps spells such as Absorb Elements from looking like they grant all listed resistances at once when they actually copy the triggering damage type. Omit only when the defensive effect has no damage-type mechanic or every listed type applies directly.

log.why: Rolled damage reduction needs runtime data separate from resistance, immunity, and outgoing damage.

fields.comment: Stores the rolled amount subtracted from qualifying damage. It maps from the structured markdown label `Damage Reduction Dice` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].damageReduction.dice preserves mechanics such as Resistance reducing damage by 1d4 without misrepresenting them as normal resistance. Omit only when the defensive effect has no rolled damage-reduction mechanic.

log.why: The runtime needs to know whether a reduction modifies incoming damage or some later outgoing damage total.

fields.comment: Stores which damage total the reduction modifies. It maps from the structured markdown label `Damage Reduction Applies To` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].damageReduction.appliesTo keeps defensive subtraction separate from attack augments and healing. Omit only when the effect has no damage-reduction mechanic.

log.why: Once-per-turn reduction limits need runtime data rather than prose-only consumption text.

fields.comment: Stores how often the damage reduction can be used while the effect is active. It maps from the structured markdown label `Damage Reduction Frequency` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].damageReduction.frequency makes limits such as `once per turn` enforceable and auditable. Omit only when the effect has no damage-reduction mechanic.

log.why: Runtime needs a resistance-suppression path for spells that remove a target's existing resistance instead of granting resistance.

fields.comment: Stores damage types whose existing resistance is suppressed on the affected target. It maps from the structured markdown label `Resistance Suppression Damage Types` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].resistanceSuppression.damageType[] preserves mechanics such as Elemental Bane making the target lose resistance to the chosen type without confusing them with normal resistance, immunity, vulnerability, or damage subtraction. Omit or use an empty array only when the effect has no resistance-suppression mechanic.

log.why: The runtime needs to know whether resistance suppression applies to every listed type, a chosen type, or a triggering damage type.

fields.comment: Stores how the active resistance-suppression damage type is selected from effects[].resistanceSuppression.damageType[]. It maps from the structured markdown label `Resistance Suppression Damage Type Source` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].resistanceSuppression.damageTypeSource keeps chosen-type suppression from looking like all eligible resistances are suppressed at once. Omit only when the effect has no resistance-suppression mechanic or every listed suppression type applies directly.

log.why: Hallow's area extra effect can grant either resistance or vulnerability and needs a neutral runtime shape.

fields.comment: Stores area or mode-based damage interaction choices. It maps from the structured markdown label `Damage Interaction Modes` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].damageInteraction.modes[] preserves Hallow-style resistance/vulnerability modes without treating vulnerability as a defense. Omit only when the effect has no damage-interaction mode.

Stores eligible damage types for a damage-interaction mode. It maps from the structured markdown label `Damage Interaction Damage Types` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].damageInteraction.damageType[] keeps chosen-type area resistance/vulnerability machine-readable. Omit only when the effect has no damage-interaction mode.

Stores how the active damage-interaction type is selected from effects[].damageInteraction.damageType[]. It maps from the structured markdown label `Damage Interaction Damage Type Source` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].damageInteraction.damageTypeSource prevents eligible choices from being mistaken for simultaneous interaction with every listed type. Omit only when the effect has no damage-interaction mode.

Stores which subjects the damage-interaction mode can affect. It maps from the structured markdown label `Damage Interaction Subject Scope` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].damageInteraction.subjectScope keeps area-mode eligibility separate from spell targeting. Omit only when the effect has no damage-interaction mode.

Stores when the damage-interaction mode applies. It maps from the structured markdown label `Damage Interaction Duration Scope` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].damageInteraction.durationScope keeps area-bound interactions from becoming permanent creature changes. Omit only when the effect has no damage-interaction mode.

log.why: Source-limited defenses need runtime source categories so resistance/immunity does not become unconditional.

fields.comment: Stores incoming source categories that a defensive effect applies to. It maps from the structured markdown label `Defense Source Categories` after that label has been checked against canonical prose. Purpose: effects[].defenseSourceFilter.sourceCategories[] preserves source restrictions such as `from attacks` without confusing them with target filters. Omit only when the defensive effect has no source-category restriction.

log.why: Nonmagical-attack-only defenses need runtime magical-status filtering.

fields.comment: Stores whether an attack-source defense applies to magical attacks, nonmagical attacks, or attacks of any magical status. Purpose: effects[].defenseSourceFilter.attackMagicalStatus keeps source-limited resistance, such as Investiture of Stone, from behaving like blanket damage resistance. Omit only when the defensive effect has no attack magical-status restriction.

acceptedValueNotes.half: Use `half` for effects[].condition.saveModifiers[].ignoredCover only when the canonical spell prose supports that exact mechanic; otherwise use a more specific value or `not_applicable` if available.

log.why: Minor Illusion's Sound and Image modes have different allowed senses, excluded senses, sound timing, and image size limits that were previously flattened into prose.

fields.why: Illusion reveal mechanics need a runtime home separate from escape checks because discerning an illusion can change only one creature's perception without ending the spell.
fields.comment: Stores how an illusion can be examined or physically revealed and what happens for creatures that discern it. log.why: Minor Illusion lets creatures Study the sound or image, reveals image mode through physical interaction, and makes the illusion faint only to discerning creatures.
